tips.tip: Nod or bow slightly when greeting
tips.detail: Handshakes are common in business and urban settings. A nod and smile is sufficient in casual situations.

tips.tip: Address people by their title and surname first
tips.detail: Use 'Mr./Ms. + Surname' or professional titles (e.g., 'Teacher Wang'). Wait to be invited to use first names.

tips.tip: Never give clocks as gifts
tips.detail: Giving a clock ('song zhong') sounds like 'attending a funeral' and symbolizes death.

tips.tip: Present and receive gifts with both hands
tips.detail: Using both hands shows respect. Gifts are often not opened immediately in front of the giver.

tips.tip: Remove your shoes before entering someone's home
tips.detail: Slippers will usually be provided. This is a sign of respect and cleanliness.

tips.tip: Wear socks when visiting homes or temples
tips.detail: Going barefoot can be seen as impolite or disrespectful.

tips.tip: Never stick chopsticks upright in a rice bowl
tips.detail: This resembles incense burned for the dead and is considered very inauspicious.

tips.tip: Use the serving utensils for shared dishes
tips.detail: Avoid using your own chopsticks to take food from communal plates unless serving utensils are provided.

tips.tip: Ask permission before taking photos of people
tips.detail: Especially in rural areas or of older individuals. Respect refusals gracefully.

tips.tip: Avoid photographing military or government buildings
tips.detail: This is often prohibited and can attract unwanted attention.

tips.tip: Use your whole hand to gesture toward people or places
tips.detail: Pointing with a single finger is considered rude. Use an open palm, chin, or head nod to indicate direction.

tips.tip: Be mindful of number symbolism
tips.detail: The number '4' (sì) sounds like 'death' (sǐ) and is avoided. '8' (bā) is considered very lucky.

tips.tip: Understand color symbolism
tips.detail: Red is lucky/joyful, used for celebrations and weddings. White and black are associated with mourning and funerals.

tips.tip: Receive a business card with both hands and study it
tips.detail: Take a moment to read the title and company. Never write on it immediately in front of the giver. Place it respectfully in a case.

tips.tip: Be patient and assertive in queues
tips.detail: Queuing culture can be less strict than in some countries. Stay calm, hold your position, and move forward when appropriate.

tips.tip: Speak at a moderate volume in public spaces
tips.detail: Loud talking, especially on public transport or in restaurants, is often noticed. Keep conversations at a considerate level.

tips.tip: Avoid public displays of anger or confrontation
tips.detail: Maintaining 'face' (miànzi) is important. Raised voices cause embarrassment and rarely resolve issues.

tips.tip: Do not spit, litter, or smoke in non-designated areas
tips.detail: These behaviors are increasingly frowned upon and can result in fines in major cities.

tips.tip: Offer your seat to the elderly, pregnant, or disabled on public transport
tips.detail: This is a deeply respected social norm and showing consideration is highly appreciated.

tips.tip: Be cautious with gift amounts and bills
tips.detail: When gifting money, use new bills and avoid amounts containing the number '4'. Even numbers (except 4) are generally preferred for gifts.
